volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Two Bacchises (Section 5.1.1100 - Section 5.2.1155b)

sections.label: Section 5.1.1100
sections.original: me hoc aétatis ludíficari , immo edepol sic ludos factum
sections.translation: that I , at my time of life , should be made a fool of , aye , by Heaven , so made a very sport of , and with my hoary head and white beard , that wretched I should be bamboozled out of my gold . Undone am I , inasmuch as this slave of mine has dared in this way to set not the value of a nutshell upon me . And I — if any other way I had lost a greater sum — I should have taken it less amiss , and have deemed it less of a loss to me .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1104
sections.original: Certo híc prope me mihi néscio quis loqui vísust ; sed quem vídeo ?
sections.translation: For sure , some person , I know not who , seems to be talking near to me . But who ’ s this I see ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.1105
sections.original: hic quidémst pater Mnesilochi .
sections.translation: Really , ’ tis the father of Mnesilochus .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1105b
sections.original: Eúge , socium aerúmnae et mei mali vídeo .
sections.translation: Hah ! I see a partner in affliction . Save you , Philoxenus !

sections.label: Section 5.1.1106b
sections.original: Et tu . unde ágis ?
sections.translation: And you ; whence are you betaking yourself ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.1106c
sections.original: Vnde homo miser átque infortunátus .
sections.translation: From a place whence comes a wretched and a luckless mortal .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1107
sections.original: At pól ego ibi sum , esse ubi míserum hominem decet átque infortunátum .
sections.translation: Why , troth , I ’ m surely on the earth , the spot where it befits a wretched and a luckless mortal to be .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1108
sections.original: Igitúr pari fortuna , aétate ut sumus , útimur .
sections.translation: We now , as we are of like age , are meeting with similar fortunes .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1108b
sections.original: Sic est . séd tu ,
sections.translation: So it is . But as to yourself , what ’ s the matter with you ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.1109b
sections.original: Pól mihi par , idem est quód tibi .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , mine ’ s the same mishap as your own .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1110
sections.original: Númquid nam ad fílium haec aégritudo áttinet ?
sections.translation: Does this misfortune in any way relate to your son ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.1111
sections.original: Ádmodum .
sections.translation: Such is the fact .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1111b
sections.original: Idém mihi mórbus in péctorest .
sections.translation: The same disease exists in my own breast .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1112
sections.original: Át mihi Chrýsalus óptumus homo
sections.translation: Aye , and that very worthy fellow , Chrysalus , has been ruining my son , myself , and all my fortunes .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1114
sections.original: Quíd tibi ex fílio nam , óbsecro , aegrést ?
sections.translation: Pray now , what is this mishap of yours about your son ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.1114b
sections.original: Scies :
sections.translation: You shall know :

sections.label: Section 5.1.1115
sections.original: íd , perit cúm tuo : ambo aéque amicás habent .
sections.translation: together with your own son he ’ s undone ; both of them are keeping mistresses alike .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1116
sections.original: Qui scís ?
sections.translation: How do you know ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.1116b
sections.original: Vidi
sections.translation: I have seen them .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1116c
sections.original: Eí mihi , dísperii .
sections.translation: Ah ! wretch that I am ! I ’ m ruined outright .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1117
sections.original: Quíd dubitamus púltare atque huc évocare ambós foras ?
sections.translation: Why do we hesitate a moment to knock and to call them both hither out of doors .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1118
sections.original: Haud moror .
sections.translation: I don ’ t object .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1118b
sections.original: Heus Bacchis , iube sis actutum aperiri fores ,
sections.translation: Hallo there ! Bacchis ! Bid the door to be opened this instant , if you please , unless you had rather the door and the posts be knocked to bits with hatchets .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1120
sections.original: Quis sónitu ac tumúltu tantó nominát me atque púltat aedes ?
sections.translation: Who is it that calls out my name with such a noise and tumult , and is knocking so hard at the door ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1121
sections.original: Ego átque hic .
sections.translation: I and this person here .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1121b
sections.original: Quid hóc est negóti nam , amábo ?
sections.translation: Pray , what ’ s the matter now ? Who has been driving these sheep [*] to us ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1122
sections.original: Ovís nos vocánt pessumaé .
sections.translation: These most shocking hussies call us sheep .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1122b
sections.original: Pastor hárum
sections.translation: Their shepherd ’ s asleep , as they come straying thus from the flock .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1124
sections.original: At pól nitent , haud sórdidae vidéntur ambae .
sections.translation: But , i ’ faith , they are not white ; they both look dirty .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1125
sections.original: Attonsae haé quidem ámbae usque súnt .
sections.translation: Because they have both been shorn just now .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1125b
sections.original: Vt vidéntur
sections.translation: How they seem to be laughing at us .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1126b
sections.original: Sine suo úsque arbitrátu .
sections.translation: Let them , just as long as they please .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1127
sections.original: Rerín ter in annó tu has tónsitari ?
sections.translation: Don ’ t you think that these sheep are shorn three times a year ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1128
sections.original: Pol hódie altera iám bis detónsa certo est .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , to-day one of them has been already shorn twice , that ’ s sure .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1129
sections.original: Vetulaé sunt , † thimiame .
sections.translation: They are old and fleeceless [*] , both of them .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1129b
sections.original: At bonás fuisse crédo .
sections.translation: But I think they have been in good plight once .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1130
sections.original: Vidén limulís , obsecró , ut intuéntur ?
sections.translation: Prithee , do you see how they are looking with a sheep ’ s eye at us ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1131
sections.original: Ecástor sine ómni arbitrór malitia ésse .
sections.translation: By my troth , I really do believe they are without any ill design .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1132
sections.original: Merito hóc nobis fít , qui quidem húc venerímus .
sections.translation: This happens to us deservedly , for having come here .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1133
sections.original: Cogántur quidem íntro .
sections.translation: Well , let them be driven in-doors to fold [*] .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1133b
sections.original: Haud sció quid eo opús sit ,
sections.translation: I don ’ t know what occasion there is for that , as they have neither milk nor wool . Let them stand as they are .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1135
sections.original: exsólvere quánti fuére , omnis frúctus
sections.translation: Of whatever value they have been , they are now out of date ; all their fruit has fallen off them by this . Don ’ t you see , how , straying unattended , they are ranging about at liberty ? Why , I fancy that they must be dumb with age ; they don ’ t bleat even , though they are absent from the rest of the flock .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1139
sections.original: stultae átque haud malaé videntur .
sections.translation: They seem both silly and worthless .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1140
sections.original: Revórtamur íntro , sorór .
sections.translation: Let ’ s return in-doors , sister .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1140b
sections.original: Ilico ámbae
sections.translation: Stay where you are , both of you ; these sheep want you .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1141
sections.original: Pródigium hoc quidémst : humana nós voce appellánt oves .
sections.translation: Why , surely this is a prodigy ; sheep are addressing us with a human voice .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1142
sections.original: Haec oves vobis malam rem magnam , quam debent , dabunt .
sections.translation: These sheep will return you the heavy and great injury which they owe to you .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1143
sections.original: Si quam debes , te condono : tibi habe , numquam abs te petam .
sections.translation: If you owe me aught , I forgive it you ; keep it to yourself ; I ’ ll never demand it of you . But what is the reason that you are threatening mischief to us ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1145
sections.original: Quia nostros agnos conclusos istic esse aiunt duos .
sections.translation: Because they say that our two lambs are shut up here .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1146
sections.original: Et praeter eos agnos meus est istic clam mordax canis :
sections.translation: And besides those lambs , my dog is there concealed that bites [*] . If they are not now produced to us and sent out of the house , we shall be furious rams ; we shall attack you forthwith .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1149
sections.original: Soror , ést quod te volo sécreto .
sections.translation: Sister , I have something to say to you in private .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1149b
sections.original: Eho , amábo .
sections.translation: How now , prithee ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1149c
sections.original: Quo illaec ábeunt ?
sections.translation: Whither are they going ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1150
sections.original: Senem illúm tibi dedo ultériorem , lepide út lenitum réddas ;
sections.translation: Sister , I give to you that old fellow that ’ s farthest off , that you may have him cleverly smoothed down ; I ’ ll now attack this other one that ’ s angry ; if we can only entice them here indoors .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1152
sections.original: Meum pénsum ego lepide accúrabo , quamquam ódiost mortem ampléxari .
sections.translation: I ’ ll manage my task with cleverness , although ’ tis to caress an old skeleton .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1153
sections.original: Facito út facias .
sections.translation: Take care and do your best .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1153b
sections.original: Taceás . tu tuom facito : égo quod dixi haud mútabo .
sections.translation: Be quiet ; do you do yours ; I ’ ll manage what I have said .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1154
sections.original: Quid illaéc illic in cónsilio duae sécreto consúltant ?
sections.translation: Why are these two women holding a council here in private ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1155
sections.original: Quid aís tu , homo ?
sections.translation: What say you , my good fellow —?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1155b
sections.original: Quid mé vis ?
sections.translation: What would you with me ?
